speaker: interviewer
text: We're all ready? OK. I'm going to need to talk to-- [INAUDIBLE] today is April 17, 1995. We're speaking with Mrs. Sonia Zyroff. I'm Marsha Blitstein. This is Bloomingdale, Illinois, USA. And this is tape four. Mrs. Zyroff, If you could continue please.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we are now at the point where I have to go, and I don't know where I'm going. I don't know what I'm going. I have been habilitated being with these people. And they were so nice to me. They  dressed me up, but they had to go on with the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they decided it was against the law to have a civilian on a military machine. But they arranged. They covered me up and put me on a truck, on a military truck. And that truck was going to Lutsk, which is the-- that was the biggest city of Volyn. And they were supposed to just bring me to that city. That city had 23,000 Jews. So maybe somebody's alive over there of mine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, in the meantime, all of this I had a good time. I'm happy I'm alive. And all of a sudden, they gave me a piece-- a document that the Russians don't stop me. They knew all about me. This-- all through getting in the city, they were always controlling everybody, who it was. They brought me to the-- there was a great big bridge, maybe-- maybe two miles long. It's called the [INAUDIBLE] Bridge. It's from-- to get in to Lutsk, you have to cross that bridge to come in. And they brought me and let me off in the beginning of that bridge. And they told me to go. All through the experience of the years and the horrors, never a tear, never. I just-- dry, no cry at all. The first time in my life, like a fountain, just crying without a stop. And I'm walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where am I going? Where am I going? [INAUDIBLE] the family was hiding me told me that the whole Jews were killed all over the world, because if I want to get married, I'll have to marry a Gentile. Can't marry a Jew no more. So I said, this was not my worry. But everybody in the world, Hitler advertised, was killed. The Jews in America-- everybody was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But those lieutenants told me that the Jews in America were not killed. They knew all about it. So I feel better there are some Jews of the world. But Hitler said that openly on the radio, and I heard it, if any Jew who survived where I was there, I am personally going to shake their hands. So I survived, and he did not shake my hand. But anyway, here I am. I'm walking in a fountain. The Russian police stops me. I show them the paper. They let me go. I haven't walked for ages. I'm walking and walking, and crying. I come nearly the end of the other bridge. I see some couple kids walking, looked like dressed like I was, you know, a little similar. Because those who crawled out from those hiding places, they don't look-- didn't look any more normal people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- step by step, we come close to each other, and closer. And we fell on each other's necks and started to cry. And one of the girls tells me, don't cry. You're coming with us. There are a few more already there. The Russians gave us a house. And they put straw on the floor that we can sleep. And there is some girl there that she's from your hometown. And her father was a Hebrew teacher who was my Hebrew teacher. So anyway, they brought me to that place. And I was with them overnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lice were that big. Some of them crawl out of the woods, sitting on the-- hidden in the woods and under the straw. So I-- different. I could take care of myself. I made a comb out of a piece of wood and combed my hair. The Hebrew teacher's girl had her hair one lump. I was sitting days to take her hair by hair apart, the way it's grown from the lice and the dirt. But anyway, I stayed there maybe two or three nights. And the-- in the Russian army was Czechoslovakian soldiers from Czechoslovakia. They joined-- Czech joined the-- the war. And there were Jews among them. So those Jews found out about us, and they came, bringing us-- stealing food and bringing us food to the place. And when I was leaving, the Czech family, she gave me a bundle of food to take. So when I came in, they didn't have nothing yet. So I tell everybody, eat what I've got.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'll never forget, a friend of mine, she just died in Israel, give me a pinch Don't give it away. You're going to starve yourself. I says, no. I'm alive now, I'm not going to starve. Kids, eat whatever I've got.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later, the Czechs came, and then they gave me a job. I worked for them helping the military, whatever they need and sewing field. So they gave me fabric. So I made the other women outfits. One of them is in Israel. One of them passed away. But I was sitting and sewing them up something that they can cover up their body to have-- to have a change.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I moved out to another family, rented a room with a Czechoslovakian family. Rented a room. And that was March, 1944, Jewish Passover. Exactly just before Passover, that we had Pesach here. And a few people that we were gathered, and we had Yiska.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that day, I'll never forget the crying. If God didn't hear our voices-- [SOBBING] everybody was alone-- one from here, one from here-- just screaming. That was the first Yiska. We didn't know when Yontif was. We didn't know nothing. But we found out that this is-- it was Pesach 1944. And so we-- we really didn't make Pesach. We didn't-- you know, you can figure the situation. So from there on, you know, I rented a room, and I lived in Lutsk for a while. And the war was still going on. The bombs were flying. The Germans left [INAUDIBLE]. We had to run in the bunker and hide. People-- the regular people were scared. I wasn't scared anymore. For me, it was already a picnic. I'm alive. I can go and hide, and nobody just wants me to be killed. So it was-- it was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we lived there until 1944, I think. '45-- The war ended in June '45, right? Yeah. So in '45, we got connected to Jewish communities, to Jewish leaders from the world. And they told us to leave. And I already met my husband there. Yeah, tell us about when you did meet your husband, when you met Mr. Zyroff. When I met him-- in Lutsk. I hate to keep you. I have so much to tell. It so happened when I moved out from the people where there was that house for everybody, I moved out to a Czechoslovakian family, just a mother and father were living. I rented a room from them because they had boys, and they went to the war. So they had lots of room. So a lot of Jewish-- now, if a Jewish man, young man, survived from the Holocaust, the Russian drafted them right away and sent them on the first front line. They knew that these Jews will fight to the last drop. They wouldn't-- they wouldn't give in. So we figured we couldn't afford that-- so little of us left alive. We have to do something about it to help. So when they were drafting them and putting them in the uniform, they used to come also. They were looking for a Jew. So a friend and I were bribing some of the soldier guards and taking them out from the-- from the headquarters where they drafted them. And dressed them in civilian clothes. And hide them. So in that big house that the Russian gave us, they were hiding him in the basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we try to make him papers, privately they can run to Poland. They can run to Germany. They're not going to be drafted in the army to go and fight the war, because we had our fights, and the hiding was enough for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, one night my friend knocks on my window for my bedroom. And she says, Sonia, open up. We have to come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: She brings in a man in the middle of the night. I haven't seen him or nothing. So I ask my Czech lady, I said, my husband is alive-- my ex-- my fiance is alive. He just came in. Let him stay overnight.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she put him in the dining room on her sofa to sleep. I didn't see him yet. And she left.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, I figured I have a responsibility to go and buy some food. I have a man to feed. So the meantime, I'm not there. He gets up and my Czech-- he's never seen me. He doesn't know nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Czech lady gets up and she says, you are Sonia's boyfriend? Oh, you're a lucky man. She's so nice. She's a good cook, and she knows how to sew. And she brags and tells him what wonderful l am. I came back. We all laugh. And he tells me the story that the Czech lady had married us already.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we stayed for while, in-- together in Lutsk. Then it came to [INAUDIBLE]. Right shortly we had to leave and go someplace. I don't know. The group found the news, and we are going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figured, all right, he's a strong, young man. I already have a bundle to carry. So he'll go with me to carry the bundles. So anyway, we were carrying that bundle long enough until we got-- we came to Germany, and we got into some friends who also came, and they stayed over in Germany. And a couple was getting married-- they live in Baltimore-- getting married that time. And my friends went after-- say, are you a single girl? You're a single boy. We couldn't find a rabbi to marry us. There was nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found a man who could do it. Dirty, filthy, on the run, we got married. They gave us a piece of paper-- I lost it on the running anyway-- that we got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we ran until December '45. We got into Berlin. And then they gave us something to eat. We hadn't eaten on the run. We had one piece of bread, and I wanted him to eat it. He was carrying the bundle. And he wanted me to eat it. And none of us ate it until we came there. We had to throw it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us soup. And they gave us bread. And then later, they send us to-- we were already married, so they gave us an apartment. They send us to displaced persons camp called Lampertheim. It's part of the state Hessen, small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you possibly spell the name of that DP camp for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lampertheim-- it's just the way it sounds. Spell it, because-- Lampertheim. Can you write that down?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lampertheim is [GERMAN]. I remember the name of the street was [GERMAN] 26. I'm old, but still sometimes something sticks, still, there So. we-- they evacuated the Germans. And they told them to leave some bedding, some dishes, and some stuff. It was not a camp. It was certain houses. So they gave us a house. And there-- my son was born there, 1946, May 30, in a Catholic hospital down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I had a doctor, a Nazi from Berlin. And during my pregnancy I didn't feel good, so his diagnosis was that I have a miscarriage. They take him out, because if I'll have something, I'll be sorry for the rest of my life. And they bring the pills with the water to me while I'm in the hospital. And somebody tells me, don't take it. I don't know who it was. Just don't take it. Stick to my mind. My mother used to say if you ever accidentally get pregnant, don't do nothing. So I just heard her say it. And I sent them back. The doctor came and threw me out of the hospital. Get out of here. I'm not--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Dr. [GERMAN] from Berlin. And we had a local doctor, by the name of Dr. Kramer. So the two doctors didn't want any part of me. So I grew. Everybody thought I'm going to have twins-- swollen hands, swollen legs. I come for a check up. So all the other-- all the women after the war started getting pregnant because we were alone, you know. So we wanted children. We wanted family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these two doctors go through. And they come to me. I am pregnant, too. You know what to expect. We told you that, scaring me. So God was with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my son, was born that-- was not easy. It was not easy. They had to-- the Catholic nuns saved my life. They wired Heidelberg. Two doctors came on motorcycles. I couldn't [INAUDIBLE] to have the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came, and they took him with instruments. It was too late for cesarean. They took him with instruments. I was sick [INAUDIBLE] for several months before.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I didn't expect a normal child. They told me whatever you expect, I'll be sorry the rest of my life. Those words must've been a German Nazi. And those women, who-- they had to cut. They wouldn't give them anesthetics. They just cut them to feel the pain and screaming their head off. And we were laying like animals. One could see what the other was doing. They wouldn't give anesthetic because that doctor was-- ran away from Berlin. He must've been a Nazi.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one woman was screaming her head off. They had to sew her up without anesthetic, without anything. Screaming her head off, but nobody ever did nothing, nothing for her. So he wanted me to kill another Jew or something. I don't why-- because I was sick during the pregnancy, which I went through so much, it was hard. But anyway, that's a separate story.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they found me, the nuns of the hospital found me on the hall floor laying alone with the baby head halfway. On the floor, unconscious, they picked me up. They brought me on the table and told me to count. So I knew I have nothing to live for. They told me to inhale as much as I can.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I inhaled so much that I couldn't wake up. I couldn't wake up. They were waiting for me to wake me up, and wake me up. And nothing doing, the whole crew was waiting. Nothing helped. Until I feel a slap in my nose and a cry. I look. There's a baby on my chest. And they-- they had nothing to dress. The baby naked. The baby's crying. It slapped my nose to wake me up. I opened my eyes, and the crew of the doctors and nurses are around my bed. And everybody is smiling. And I say, whose is that? It's a big, fat pig, and he's all yours. He tell me in the German language, it's a big, fat pig, and he's all yours. And he's all right. He weighed almost 10 pounds. So anyway, this was another miracle story. There they want to take the baby and kill me, probably with him. So I survived that one. And from that time on, I came home from the hospital. I find a letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the letter reads, I am [INAUDIBLE], your cousin from Montreal. I know you survived. I heard. I'm sending out letters, like Noah sent out the doves from the ark, all over Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to find you. So I got that letter. Can you imagine how I felt? A first cousin of mine-- she was a librarian. Her husband was a Jewish writer who wrote that article for me in the paper. And then she found out the family in Duluth. I had other relatives on my father's side. The-- the-- the brother did not live anymore, but five children is married.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all took interest, pitched in, sent us the papers to Germany. And we left Germany in 1947. We got in to Duluth, Minnesota, December, Christmas Eve, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're in this country with your husband and you son.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my husband and my son, 18 months old. The biggest devil in the world. On the boat, I was sick. I had to land with him in a hospital, because I couldn't control him. He was on the run all the time. He ran all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah, and then without knowing the language, we traveled all the way from New York, changing trains. There's no planes at that time. There was times that they filled up the train, that we had to stay all night, hold the baby on the train overnight, coming out from the displaced person camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in the displaced person camp from-- we got-- we got there December in '45. And we left December, '47. I was supposed to go on the Exodus boat. My husband was active in that deal. The Exodus boat-- they don't accept me because I had a baby. My husband wanted to go to Israel. He didn't want to go to-- to the States. But my family insists. That cousin of mine wrote a letter to him and say, look, if you're from America-- Israel, the situation is very bad. Israel in a state of war. You go to the family in America. If you-- any time-- from America, you can go to Israel any time. So that convinced him. We went on a truck to go to American consul. He was booed by the [INAUDIBLE] group. He was active, like [INAUDIBLE] group. He was active in that organization. He was mad at me because he didn't want to go to America. He was booed by his-- by his marching group in Germany, demanding to give us our homeland. He was active in that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you arrived in this country, what were your first impressions? What did you think of the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I think of the US-- number one, I came in Christmas Eve. And I saw all of these decorations. And I thought, what in the world was that? So when I arrived at a train, the family arranged to come pick us up in Duluth to the train station. There is a man with a car with a uniform with a fancy hat. I nearly had a heart attack. I thought, well, the Nazis followed me. What in the world is he? It happened to be my cousin's son, a cadet from West Point, came home on leave. So he came with the car to pick us up. But then there was other-- two more cars with relatives. They came to pick us up. And a cousin of mine in Duluth kept us for a couple of weeks at her house, until they found a house. And they paid for one month's rent. And when that month ran out, we went to an uncle's basement. So we were in the basement without water, without warm water, without a refrigerator. And we struggled a little bit in America. We-- we didn't have it like some other people who come nowadays, that they give them housing, they give them everything. We had to start from scratch without a penny, without a penny. One day my cousin said, bring your laundry to wash to my house. And I will never forget. The bus ride was $0.10. And a quart of milk was $0.10. I decided to walk maybe 20 blocks to my cousin's house way in the east end to save the $0.10 for the quart of milk. I froze up my knees. And she got mad at me, scolding me why I didn't take the bus. It was 20 below zero in February. And I walked that far with a bundle. That was only one of the incidents. We struggled bitterly. My husband shoveled coal for the Minnesota Power and Light Company. Then he's got a job. He lost that one. He got a job. He did not know the language. Then he got a job to carry the junk in a junkyard, just carry, loading trains, loading trucks. That was his labor work. But he did go to-- to school. I couldn't go. I had to be home with the baby, because a babysitter had to cost $0.25 an hour. So I couldn't go to school. So I just send him to school to learn the language to have a job. And he worked himself up in the same field. And he came a part boss down there in the business. And he worked there for 37 years. And right now my husband's at a nursing home, and he's dying at this point. We were waiting. My son is going with me home. We were waiting. At least he held up for me to go away. So he's living on water already, six weeks-- just an intravenous, nothing else. And he's damaged from the Holocaust, in my opinion. He always talked about it, screaming nights. He was a young boy. He's younger than I. He was a young boy after the war. He was damaged from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your husband choose to talk about his experiences when he came?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. Screaming, blood was running, they're killing Jews until he wakes up.

speaker: interviewer
text: But to others, the way that you want to talk to others when you first got to this country. Did he want to talk about his experiences as you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. He didn't have time for that. He just worked, constantly go to-- we had a responsibility, me and a baby. And-- and so he didn't do that. I did most of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you wanted to talk about it, were people willing to listen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The non-Jews. I'm ashamed to say that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Unless it was leaders of an organization that they had to know. Otherwise, it was just shush, shush, shush. We don't want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that way because they labeled me that I talked too much about the Holocaust. That's why I'm not good enough. They don't want to hear about it. It came back to me. That's the truth, I'm ashamed to admit, but that is the truth. And I felt I'm going to say how I feel as long as I live. I'll never forget it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by word of mouth, I started out right into churches. And they were sending me presents, flowers, and stuff like that, and contributions to my favorite organization. I never took nothing for myself. So the Unitarian Church was the first one. And somehow the others, they wanted a program or something. To every church, almost, in town I was there. I was there, unless they were anti-Semitic leaders. They didn't want to hear about it. Now, these are groups you're talking about. What about individuals you'd meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: If they asked me, I'd tell them. I just tell them. I tell them I lost my whole family. And I'll never forget, and I'll never forgive. One time I was scheduled to speak at Saint Scholastica, which is a Catholic school. They told me. They made an appointment with me. I was to speak that day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a day before and nun calls me up. Under one condition we let you talk, if you'll say to our children that you will forget and forgive. I said, Sister, no way. I'm not going to say that. If your family would be all killed, would you want to forget that? I will never forget and never forgive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was way before yet, that we went to Israel in '81-- I went to Israel, and the second generation from all over the world-- it was a Holocaust survivors gathering in '81 in Jerusalem. And at the Wailing Wall, leaders of the second generation groups swore at the Wailing Wall to accept that slogan-- we must never forget, never forgive, and never again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm sticking by it from that on. It gave me a lot of support, that I'm not odd. I just don't want-- some others say, hey, I don't want to talk about. I want to forget about it. I don't want talk about it. It's not me. I'm going to talk about it. I had a wonderful family, wonderful mother and father. Why? I'm still asking that question. Yesterday, too, was a speaker. Here at the synagogue, they talk about the religion, religion, Hasidism, religion, religion, Orthodox. All right, all right, religion-- my question, where was my God? Just tell me that.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were more religious than any American. European Jews were religious. But the religious speakers of the United States go around telling-- there was a speaker in Duluth. And I went there. And he was saying they were killing European Jews because they were sinners. European Jews were sinners.

speaker: interviewee
text: In my book, there's more sinners in America. Some of my relatives didn't want to go to America because they figured that the doorknob is [NON-ENGLISH] in America. See? They didn't want to go. So why should I, you know, not talk about it and not saying anything? Those who want to listen, will listen. Those who don't want, they don't have to. If they don't ask me, I wouldn't say. You talk about God in relation to the Holocaust. How observant are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: See, my son this one? Orthodox, very orthodox-- bought a condominium next to the shul. They live in a gorgeous home in the mountains. Friday afternoon, they check into the-- to their condominium. And they go home Sunday. They shouldn't drive the car on shabbat. [INAUDIBLE] He was conservative, like we were. We came. We were beginning orthodox. Then the orthodox synagogue didn't provide nothing for children. They had nothing for kids. I wanted my kids to have a Hebrew education. I wanted my kids to go to Jewish camps. And I wanted them to be with Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I couldn't belong to the orthodox. I'm still belonging. I'm paying some dues there, the sisterhood. But the conservative at that time had a nice rabbi, nice [INAUDIBLE]. And the kids had a lot of activities.

speaker: interviewee
text: My kids were on Hebrew school scholarships almost every year to go to camp. They not only excelled in the English. They excelled in the Hebrew school, too. [INAUDIBLE] my son now from the Duluth Hebrew school, from the Duluth rabbi education, he now conducts services on Rosh Hashanah, the first day in an orthodox synagogue in La Jolla, California. Yesterday, at the first day, he read the-- read the hof Torah and the Torah reading during the services here, the first day of Pesach. So in a way, I'm proud of it. But to be honest, if that's what he was making for a living, I wouldn't be so happy. But if he makes a living as being a specialist, or doctor, to do something extra in the religious field, I am proud of it. So I fly in each time. But the only problem, as I told you, I don't know if I should say it on the-- I will say it on the tape, because it bothers me, bitterly, that the rabbi, the shul he goes and belongs and supports and gives tells me that he doesn't observe the Holocaust. He combines it with [HEBREW]. That is killing me. That is killing me. And the problem is it bothers me more so that he has all the young people. The whole synagogue is only young people. You wouldn't find a real old person of my age. No-- not one Holocaust survivor is a member of that synagogue.
